eyebrow: CSAI@LIVE · DEEP LEARNING · L8 · ATTENTION & TRANSFORMERS
title: ATTENTION & TRANSFORMERS
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 8 (Self-Attention, Transformers & Positional Encoding)

sections.title: ATTENTION = CONTEXT, NOT MEMORY
sections.exam: Attention's job is to capture contextual relationships between tokens; in seq2seq it builds a context vector = a weighted sum of encoder states, weighted by a query–key compatibility score.
sections.ascii:
decoder asks:  q (query) ─┐
                          ▼
 encoder states:  h1    h2    h3    h4     (keys / values)
 score(q,hi):    0.1   0.7   0.1   0.1     ← query·key compatibility
                  │     │     │     │
                softmax  →  weights α (sum to 1)
                          ▼
 context = Σ αi · hi   ── weighted sum, here mostly h2

 NOT recurrence · NOT gating · NOT convolution — just selective focus.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Each step can attend to the previous steps and itself — the seed of self-attention: a token weighs the others by relevance.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The attention matrix between encoder and decoder states. The context vector is a weighted sum of encoder hidden states — the exam answer for 'how is the context vector computed'.

sections.title: SELF-ATTENTION: Q, K, V & SCALED DOT-PRODUCT
sections.exam: scores = Q·Kᵀ / √d_k — dot-product similarity between the ROWS of Q and K, scaled by √(key-dim), softmax over the last dim, then weighted sum of V; the scores tensor is (batch, seq_len, seq_len).
sections.ascii:
each token x ──► Q = x·Wq   K = x·Wk   V = x·Wv   (3 learned Linear maps)

 scores = Q·Kᵀ        dot-product similarity (row i vs row j)
        ÷ √d_k        scale by sqrt(key dim) → stable softmax
        softmax(-1)   → attention weights, each row sums to 1
 z = weights · V      → context-aware output

 shapes:  x (B, n, d) ─► scores (B, n, n) ─► z (B, n, d)
 highest weight ⇒ that token's V vector dominates the output.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Words → embeddings → Q/K/V → scores → weights → output as a weighted sum of values. The whole self-attention pipeline in one row.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The exam formula itself: Q times K-transpose, divided by root d_k, softmaxed, times V. Note Q and K are stacked as ROWS.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Queries × keys gives the score grid (before softmax) — one similarity per query–key pair across the sentence.

head: Self-attention code
What it does
rows: map tokens into a shared space to compute attention
updated token reps, weighted by attention to all tokens

sections.title: MULTI-HEAD ATTENTION
sections.exam: Split the embedding into h heads (head_dim = embed_size // heads, so embed_size MUST be divisible by heads), run scaled dot-product attention in each head in parallel, Concat, then project with Wo.
sections.ascii:
embed_size d, heads h  ⇒  head_dim = d // h   (must divide evenly!)
 einsum 'nqhd,nkhd->nhqk'  =  per-head query·key scores

 x ─┬─► head1:  softmax(Q1·K1ᵀ/√dk)·V1 ─┐
    ├─► head2:  ...                     ├─► Concat ─► × Wo ─► out (d)
    └─► head h:  ...                     ┘
       heads run in parallel, each sees a d//h slice → different relations.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: V, K, Q each pass through Linear, h scaled-dot-product attentions run in parallel, then Concat and a final Linear (Wo).

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Inputs split into 3 attention heads; each output is concatenated and multiplied by the weight matrix Wo to give the final output.

sections.title: POSITIONAL ENCODING
sections.exam: Transformers have NO built-in order, so ADD a fixed (p, d) matrix of sin/cos values over exponentially increasing wavelengths; low dims change fast (local/word-level), high dims change slow (global/doc-level); not learned, generalizes to arbitrary length.
sections.ascii:
Transformer has NO sense of order ⇒ ADD a position signal

 PE(pos, 2i)   = sin( pos / 10000^(2i/d) )   ← even dims
 PE(pos, 2i+1) = cos( pos / 10000^(2i/d) )   ← odd  dims

 low  dim i ── high frequency ── changes FAST → local / word-level
 high dim i ── low  frequency ── changes SLOW → global / document-level

 input = token-embedding  +  PE   (PE is a fixed p×d matrix, any length)

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Rows = position, columns = dimension i. Left columns oscillate fast (high freq, local), right columns oscillate slowly (low freq, global) — exactly the exam's low-vs-high dimension contrast.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Full Transformer: positional encoding is summed onto the input/output embeddings at the bottom — order is injected, not hard-wired like in an RNN.

sections.title: ADD & NORM + FEED-FORWARD
sections.exam: Each sublayer is wrapped in a residual Add then LayerNorm; LayerNorm takes mean/variance over the FEATURE dimension of a single example, so it is batch-independent — the right choice for Transformers; a position-wise feed-forward follows.
sections.ascii:
┌───────────────────────► (residual / skip)
 in ──────►│ Self-Attention or FF │──► + ──► LayerNorm ──► out
          └───────────────────────

 LayerNorm: mean & var over the FEATURE dim of ONE example
   → batch-independent (works on 1 token at a time → used in Transformers)
 BatchNorm: stats over the mini-batch → noisy on tiny batches
 Feed-Forward: same MLP applied position-wise (each token independently).

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: LayerNorm formula with the note 'statistics are taken over the last dimension' — i.e. across features of one example, not across the batch.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Every Multi-Head Attention and Feed-Forward block is followed by an Add & Norm; stacked N times in both encoder and decoder.

sections.title: SELF, MASKED & CROSS ATTENTION
sections.exam: Encoder self-attention is bidirectional; decoder MASKED self-attention draws Q,K,V all from the decoder with a causal mask (attend only to earlier target tokens); encoder–decoder CROSS-attention takes Q from the decoder and K,V from the encoder.
sections.ascii:
1) ENCODER self-attn      Q,K,V ← encoder    (bidirectional, see all)
2) DECODER masked self-attn  Q,K,V ← decoder (causal: only earlier tokens)
3) CROSS-attention        Q ← decoder ; K,V ← encoder (read the source)

 causal mask — keep lower-tri, set upper-tri to −∞ before softmax:
          k1    k2    k3
   q1 [   ✓    −∞    −∞ ]
   q2 [   ✓     ✓    −∞ ]
   q3 [   ✓     ✓     ✓ ]
 BERT=encoder(MLM) · GPT=decoder(causal) · T5/BART=encoder-decoder(seq2seq)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The decoder block's middle sublayer is cross-attention: its queries come from the decoder, its keys/values from the encoder output.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Future positions get −∞ added before softmax, so a decoder position can only attend to itself and earlier tokens — masked self-attention.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Decoder stack → Linear → Softmax → argmax over the vocabulary: how a token is finally generated, one step at a time.

sections.title: WHY TRANSFORMERS BEAT RNNs
sections.exam: RNNs process one token at a time (sequential → hard to parallelize); Transformers process all positions in parallel (fast for short n) but self-attention is O(n²); both aim to capture long-distance dependencies; Transformer embeddings are contextual, Word2Vec are static.
sections.ascii:
RNN:   x1 ─► x2 ─► x3 ─► x4     sequential · O(n) · hard to parallelize
 Trans:  x1  x2  x3  x4          all positions at once → parallel, fast
         └── all-pairs ──┘        but self-attention cost ~ O(n²)

 both: capture long-distance dependencies in sequences
 embeddings:
   Word2Vec     → ONE fixed vector for 'bank' (context-free)
   Transformer  → context-dependent vector (river-bank vs money-bank)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Decoder training: input is the target shifted right (SOS ... ), target ends in EOS — autoregressive, one token at a time.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Self-attention turns static input embeddings into context-aware outputs — why a Transformer's 'bank' differs by sentence while Word2Vec's does not.
